revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Amabe clan]][[fr:Clan Amabé]][[ja:海部氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese noble family|qq=}} [[File:Amabe Clan genealogy.jpg|thumb|{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu||qq=}} a national treasure]] '''Amabe clan (海部氏)''' Is a Japanese clan associated with [[Kono Shrine]], which they have run since the [[Kofun period]].<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese|qq=}}</ref> The clan was originally the [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] or provincial governors of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[Kono Shrine]],<ref name="Bunka1" /> they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-11-01 |title=Kuni no miyatsuko {{!|qq=}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |access-date=2023-11-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231101031652/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-11-01 |qq=}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
They are not to be confused with the {{ill|Ama People|qid=Q9495537}} or the {{ill|Amabe Caste|ja|海人部|qid=Q55528867}}
== Genealogy == The {{nihongo|Amabe clan genealogy|海部氏系図|Amabe-shi Keizu||qq=}} is a famous document housed at Kono Shrine. It is from early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan. The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango. The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.<ref name="Bunka1" /> In ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}'', the descendants of [[Amatsuhikone]], [[Ame no Hohi]], and {{ill|Amanomichine|ja|天道根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanomichine|qq=}}, together with the descendants of Amenohoakari are referred to as ''Tenson-zoku''. The ''Tenson-zoku'' descended from [[Takamagahara]] (Plain of High {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|qid=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}) to {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari|1=Owari Province}} and {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}} provinces, and are considered to be the ancestors of Owari clan, {{ill|Tsumori clan|lt=Tsumori|ja|津守氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsumori clan|qq=}}, Amabe clan, and {{ill|Tanba clan|lt=Tanba|ja|丹波氏|1=Tanba clan|2=ja|3=丹波氏|4=ko|5=단바씨|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba clan|qq=}} clans.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hanawa |first=Hokiichi |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/959773242 |title=Shinsen Shōjiroku (新撰姓氏錄) |date=1983 |publisher=Onkogakkai |location=Japan |oclc=959773242|qq=}}</ref> However, Toshio Hoga argues that ''Amabe clan genealogy'', which records these four clans as descendants of Amenohoakari, is a forged document,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Kokuho「Amabe-shi Keizu」he no gimon, Kokigi no Heya (国宝「海部氏系図」への疑問 古樹紀之房間) |year=2006 |location=Japan|qq=}}</ref> and that these clans actually descended from the {{ill|List of water deities|en|List of water deities|qid=Q13416336|lt=sea deity|1=List of water deities}} {{ill|Watatsumi|en|Watatsumi|qid=Q1474187|lt=Watatsumi|1=Watatsumi}}. In addition, Owari clan's genealogy includes the great-grandson of Watatsumi, [[Takakuraji]], as their ancestor, and he argues this is the original genealogy.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Tango no Amabe-shi no Shutsuji to sono ichizoku, Kokigi no Heya (丹後の海部氏の出自とその一族) |year=2006 |location=Japan|qq=}}</ref> ==References== {{reflist|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|海部氏|version=101918815|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[en:Amabe clan]]
[[ja:Amabe clan]]
<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|||qq=}}, early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図|||qq=}}, early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Amabe clan}} [[fr:Clan Amabé]] [[ja:海部氏]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Amabe clan}} [[fr:Clan Amabé]] [[ja:海部氏]] {{wikidata link|Q65272445}} [[Category:丹後国の氏族]] [[Category:Clans in Shikoku region]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:近畿地方の氏族]] [[Category:阿波国の氏族]]
